narrative:
Short impact summary

The software tool Effsize, authored by Marco Torchiano and made available through Zenodo, has garnered significant academic attention, reflected in its 320 citations. With an impressive 7,871 views and 355 downloads recorded on DataCite, it demonstrates its utility and relevance within the research community.

Full narrative

Effsize, a software tool developed by Marco Torchiano at the Polytechnic University of Turin, stands out in the realm of effect size computation since its release in 2016. Its influence is evidenced by a solid citation count of 320, illustrating how it has been embraced by researchers across various disciplines, particularly in psychology and biological sciences.

The significant engagement with Effsize is further highlighted by the tool's online usage metrics, which indicate it has been viewed 7,871 times and downloaded 355 times from the DataCite repository. These metrics signal a healthy level of active community reuse and technical value, showcasing its importance as a foundational research output in the field.

Moreover, the tool's reach extends internationally, with top citing countries including the United States (116 citations), Germany (69), and the United Kingdom (49). This geographical diversity underscores the global relevance of the software and its integral role in enhancing research methodologies across various academic institutions.

Effsize is also notable for its early sharing through preprint avenues, as it has successfully generated 1 citation in its preprint form. This practice of open science ensures immediate community engagement, facilitating ongoing dialogue and advancements in the effective computation of effect sizes long before formal publication.
